title: What Are Blue Light Glasses? A Clear Explanation of How They Work
description: Everything you need to know about blue light glasses: what they are, how the lenses work, who they're for, and whether you actually need them. Plain-English guide.
tldr: Blue light glasses have lenses that filter a portion of blue-violet light (380–450 nm) from screens, LED lights, and sunlight. They're available as prescription or non-prescription and typically have a subtle yellow tint. The science on their benefits is mixed — modest sleep improvements, but minimal proven effect on eye strain.

definition.text: Eyeglasses equipped with lenses that absorb or reflect a percentage of high-energy visible (HEV) blue light, particularly in the blue-violet spectrum (380–450 nm). The filtering is achieved through specialized coatings applied to the lens surface or materials embedded within the lens itself.

sections.content:
Blue light glasses are regular eyeglasses (with or without a prescription) that have a coating or material designed to block some blue light. You can get them as standalone non-prescription glasses for screen use, or as an add-on coating on your regular prescription glasses.

They became hugely popular during the pandemic when screen time spiked. The pitch was simple: screens emit blue light, blue light causes problems, these glasses block it. The reality is more nuanced than the marketing, but the product itself is straightforward.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. sections.h2: How Blue Light Filtering Actually Works
sections.content:
Two main technologies exist:

Coating-based filtering: A thin multi-layer coating is applied to the front lens surface. This coating reflects a portion of blue light wavelengths, similar to how anti-reflective coatings work but targeting specific wavelengths. You can sometimes see a faint blue-purple reflection on these lenses. Most affordable blue light glasses use this method.

Material-based filtering: The blue light absorbing property is built into the lens material itself. The lens has a slight yellow tint throughout because the filtering pigment is embedded in the substrate, not just the surface. These tend to filter more blue light but have a more noticeable tint.

Both approaches selectively reduce transmission of light in the approximately 380–450 nm range while allowing most other visible light through. Higher-filtration lenses block more blue light but have a warmer (more yellow) appearance.

Blue light is part of the visible light spectrum, sitting between violet and green. It has shorter wavelengths (380–500 nm) and higher energy than red, orange, or yellow light. The sun is by far the largest source of blue light — screens, LEDs, and fluorescent lights produce it too, but at much lower intensity.

Blue light isn't inherently bad. During the day, it boosts alertness, helps regulate your sleep-wake cycle, and elevates mood. The concern is specifically about excessive blue light exposure at night (which may disrupt sleep) and prolonged intense exposure (which lab studies suggest could potentially affect retinal cells — though at intensities far beyond what screens produce).

Your phone or computer emits roughly 0.1–0.5% of the blue light that the sun does. This context matters when evaluating whether filtering screen blue light is medically necessary.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. sections.h2: Who Might Benefit From Blue Light Glasses
sections.content:
Evening screen users: If you use phones, tablets, or computers within 2 hours of bedtime and have trouble falling asleep, blue light filtering (from glasses or device settings) may help. The sleep connection has the most supporting evidence.

People with migraine or photophobia: Some migraine sufferers find that filtering certain light wavelengths reduces trigger frequency. This is a specific medical consideration worth discussing with your optometrist during an eye exam.

Anyone who finds the warm tint comfortable: Subjective comfort matters. If blue light lenses feel easier on your eyes during screen work, the modest cost is worthwhile regardless of what clinical studies say about population-level effects.

People who already need glasses: If you're buying new prescription lenses anyway, adding blue light filtering is an inexpensive add-on ($30–$80). There's no downside, and you may get minor benefits.

sections.h2: Blue Light Glasses vs. Screen Night Mode
sections.content:
Your phone, tablet, and computer all have built-in blue light reduction settings (Night Shift on Apple, Night Light on Windows, etc.). These shift the display colour temperature warm, reducing blue light output at the source.

Blue light glasses filter blue light from all sources simultaneously — every screen, overhead LEDs, and ambient light. Night mode only affects one device at a time.

For sleep purposes, both achieve similar results. Night mode is free and always available. Blue light glasses cost $30–$80 but work across all visual inputs. Many people use both for belt-and-suspenders coverage in the evening.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: How to Get Blue Light Glasses
sections.content:
Non-prescription: Available from Amazon, drugstores, and optical retailers. Quality varies enormously — cheap non-prescription blue light glasses may have poor optical clarity, inconsistent filtering, and uncomfortable frames. Budget $20–$60 for decent quality.

Prescription add-on: Ask your optician to add blue light coating when ordering new lenses. This is the recommended route for anyone who wears glasses already — you get the filtering on lenses you'd be wearing anyway. Most insurance plans cover the base lenses; the blue light add-on may or may not be covered depending on your plan.

Prescription-specific: Order dedicated blue light glasses for screen use with a slightly different prescription optimized for your working distance. Your optometrist can prescribe this if you spend most of your day on screens. Same-day single vision options make this quick and easy.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Are blue light glasses the same as computer glasses?
faqs.a: Not exactly. Computer glasses have lenses optimized for intermediate distance (screen range, about 50–70 cm), which may or may not include blue light filtering. Blue light glasses filter blue light but may not have a distance-optimized prescription. You can combine both — ask your optometrist about computer-distance lenses with blue light coating.

faqs.q: Can you tell if glasses have blue light filtering?
faqs.a: Most blue light glasses have a subtle blue-purple reflection on the lens surface when you tilt them in light. Lenses with embedded filtering (material-based) may have a faint yellow tint. Some retailers include a blue light test card with purchase that shows the difference.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses work for gaming?
faqs.a: Gaming typically involves extended screen time in dimmer rooms — both conditions where blue light glasses might offer marginal comfort benefits. Many gamers report subjective comfort, especially during late-night sessions. Whether that's the blue light filtering or the anti-reflective coating at work is debatable.

faqs.q: How much do blue light glasses cost?
faqs.a: Non-prescription blue light glasses range from $15–$60 CAD online, with quality varying widely. As a prescription add-on, blue light coating typically costs $30–$80 on top of your lens cost. Some premium lens packages include it bundled with anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
